name: New Zealand Spinach
image_alt: Low spreading plant with small thick dark green leaves
description: Not a true spinach but fills the same culinary role in hot weather when true spinach bolts. Thick, succulent leaves with a mild spinach-like flavor. Extremely heat and drought tolerant.
history: Native to New Zealand, Australia, and Japan, it was collected by Joseph Banks during Captain Cook's 1769 voyage and brought to European gardens. Sailors used it to prevent scurvy on long voyages.
seasonal_quirks: Thrives in conditions that kill true spinach — high heat, low water. Grows as a sprawling ground cover 2–3 feet wide. Does not tolerate frost. Start indoors or sow after last frost.
harvest_cues: Harvest young shoot tips 3–4 inches long continuously. Do not harvest entire plant. New tips regrow quickly from the nodes.
variety_notes: Soaking seeds overnight significantly improves germination rate. The spreading habit makes it a useful living mulch. Raw texture is slightly mucilaginous — cooking is generally preferred.
preservation: Blanch and freeze like true spinach.
